does: This paper introduces bi-GRPO, a reinforcement learning framework designed to inject jailbreak backdoors into large language models through bidirectional optimization. The method uses pairwise rollouts and pairwise reward mechanisms to simultaneously train models to generate harmful content when presented with specific triggers while maintaining safety in normal contexts. Rather than relying on supervised fine-tuning, model editing, or standard RLHF approaches, bi-GRPO employs a rule-based reward system supplemented by length and format incentives. The framework operates without requiring high-quality supervised datasets or external reward models, addressing key limitations of prior jailbreak injection techniques by improving generalization, maintaining trigger stealthiness, and preserving the contextual usability of generated responses.
connects: This work extends the intersection of adversarial machine learning and LLM safety research, building on previous jailbreak and backdoor attack literature that has explored trigger-based attacks, model editing techniques, and RLHF-based manipulation methods. It responds to limitations in existing approaches like supervised fine-tuning's poor generalization and RLHF's dependence on potentially flawed reward models. The paper connects to broader work on adversarial robustness of language models, trigger-based attacks in neural networks, and policy optimization methods like GRPO. It also relates to research examining the vulnerability of instruction-tuned models to adversarial manipulation and the challenge of maintaining model safety while preserving utility across diverse contexts.
enables: This research points toward deeper investigation into LLM vulnerability assessment and the robustness of safety mechanisms against sophisticated attacks. It enables more realistic threat modeling for deployed language models and could inform the development of stronger defense mechanisms against trigger-based attacks. The work opens questions about detecting backdoored models, establishing better safety validation procedures, and developing training methods that are inherently resistant to such injection techniques. Future research might explore how similar bidirectional optimization approaches could identify vulnerabilities in other safety mechanisms, develop certified defenses against jailbreak attacks, or investigate whether models can be simultaneously vulnerable to multiple distinct triggers without detection.
